CURRENT AFFAIRS,

There ought to be a run on Shaw’s Limerick canned
meats, ctc., in view of the awful accounts from America.
As Shaws say in a newspaper advertisement: “ No dark
secrets. We belong to no Trust.”” A representative
of the Independent—is the Freeman asleep t—paid a visit
to Shaw’s bacon curing and sausage factory. Tho news-
paper man went through the sausage department, and
observed the turning out of canned meats. It will be
remembered that an Irish-Canadian, in tho course of an
article in the LEADER, recently said:—“I noted an ad-
vertisement of Messrs. Shaw and Sons in the LEADER ;
these people stated that their goods were Irish manufac-
ture. I was fitting out a vessel for a Canadian firm, so
I sent them a trial order on the strength of what they
said in the LEADER; the order was only for £16 worth

f goods—hams, canned meats, and preserved fruits—

- and I must say that I never tasted as good, and this is

the verdict of every person who was fortunate enough to
sample them.” K

Canned meats aro a modern necessity, and now that
the American rovelations have alarmed and disgusted
the public all we hope is that Messrs. Shaw, and any
other Irish firm that may go into the industry, will be
able to meet the demands upon their resources. Even
tho bitterest of anti-Trish Irishmen would at the pre-
sent time be inclined to give not only a trial but a pre-
ference to canned meat canned by Shaws,

Sergeant Sullivan is not the only man for whom the
hat is going round in Buttevant, in “Rebel ”’ Cork.
There is a “Saved’’ bank clerk being removed, or
about to be removed, from Buttevant to a larger sphere
of “usefulness’” in Nenagh, in “ Gallant’’ Tip. As
the “ banker ’’ is going on promotion, it is fitting that
the millionaires of such a world-famous industrial
centre as Buttevant should put their hands in their
pockets, and buy an illuminated address’’ for the
nation-maker. The only thing that we find it difficult
to reconcile with the industrial status of Buttevant is
that subscriptions to the testmonial to this
bank clerk are not to exceed one guinea. Is it not
rather hard to so curb the generosity of the Buttevant
“hell of a fellow.”” One P. Roche is sec., and “ Dear
Sir (or Madam)”’ may send in the guinea to him.
Thomas W, Lenahan, J.P., is treasurer, and the Com-
mitteo comprise Rev. James Coghlan, C.C., W. R.
Oliver, Herbert Taylor, James Roche, Rev. W. H.
Cotter, M.A., LL.D.; W. J. Coleman, Joseph Byrne,
and Daniel Sheehan. Strange to say the touting cir-
cular does not mention the name of the bank in the
service of which Mr. Hill, the “banker,”’ has earned
the gratitude of Buttevant, if not of the nation. We
would like a list of the subscribers to this laudable
enterprise, for we feel sure that everyone who gives a
guinea to Mr. Hill would be only too glad to subscribe
£100 to the projected new boot company, and £200 each
to the contemplated Irish insurance company.

How fares the Sergeant Sullivan testimonial in Butte-
vant, in “Rebel”’ Cork? We understand. that Ser-
geant Sullivan added to the dignity and glory of the
nation by prosecuting a man at Christmas for the lar-
ceny of a turkey. ~We wonder is it the Buttevant
geese who are getting up the testimonial. It is said
that a Tommy Atkins prisoner escaped out of his cus-
tody. There are thirty-three Bungs in the small, but
thirsty, town of Buttevant. Perhaps it is no wonder
that the sergeant is getting a testimonial.

Even in tho maftter of tricks for inducing people to
buy papers that they do not want to read, Green, an
organ of Humphrey’s Dump, is imitating England.
Evidently some British paper started a trick for ‘n-
creasing its circulation—or rather for creating a bogus
circulation—br offering money for tram tickets. Now
oven Trolly Pole adopted this trick, as it was evidently
not equal to thinking out a trick of its own. ~ We see
by Buff—a paper that does not love Green—that the
life of one seven-years-old girl in England has already
been sacrificed to this latest trick for increasing the sale
of “literature.”” We read in the Press report:—In
summing up, the Coroner said it scemed to him a great
pity that journals whose object, he supposed, was to
increase their circulation, should hold out offers which
caused children to expose themselves to these dangers.
He understood the mabter was going to be brought
before Parliament, but whether the practice would be
stopped remained to be seen. In returning a verdict
of ‘accidental death,” the jury added a rider con-
demning the practice of certain newspapers in offering
prizes for 'bus tickets, and asked the Coroner to bring
the matter before the Home Secretary.”’ i

All the crowned heads of Europe missed a treat by
not attending the annual general meeting of the Clon-
gowes Union on Sunday last. Matty Bodkin, leader-
yuter ,for the Deplorable, and world-famous author of

Pat o’ Nine Tails,”” was present; so also was the band
of the 11th Hussars in, as the Deplorable has it, * their
gherry-coloured combinations.’’ Perhaps Matty was
in a Robert Emmet costume. - We wonder was it Matty
who wrote the description in the Deplorable? Who bub
a world-renowned litteratour could write, “ And when
Sa_llms was reached the country was bathed in the
Bngh(‘, sunshine of summer?’ Fresh and original, eh?

Cars were plentiful, and the Dublin visitors had not
the least trouble in adjusting themselves in conveniont
vel_ucles.f’ Well, now! We wonder how did Matty
adjust himself in a convenient vehiclo, for we do not
read that any donkeys with panniers were present. We
further read, “nature was in a gencrous and green
mood.”  Wo suppose tame nature adjusted itself to the
occasion—a green mood for a green union! A branch
of the Catholic Defence Society was not started at the
Clongowes Union.  What a pity that conscientious
seruples stand in the way of the members of the Clon-
gowes Union turning “ Saved.” But for that the cup of
social bliss might overflow, and the band of the 11th
Hussars might give place to the Harold’s Cross Salva-
tion Army band.

A Nenagh reader writes: —“ Your Nenagh correspon-
dent is not accurate in his observations on the Killaloe
Quarry Workingmen’s Club. The majority on the
Council of Management of the club as at present con-
stituted are Catholics. Catholic literature and Catholic
magazines will form portion of the club reading. Lite-
rature of a non-Catholic tone is excluded; the parish
priest is a trustee of the hall. If Bung had a look in
at its inception, the incident is regrettable, but we
know Bung is almighty in this land of ours To non-
Catholics, indeed, must be given the credit of its incep-
tion, but they had the full and entire approval and
concurrence of the parish priest at the time. The end
and objects for which the club is established should
commend themselves to all classes—viz., to promoto
temperance amongst the employees by catering for them
only on temperance lines, and lightening the burden of
their daily toil by supplying a commodious, well-fur-
nished and well-appointed hall where they can enjoy
their daily meal and evening cup in comfort.  Your
correspondent need not fear for Catholic interests there,
they are absolutely and entirely safeguarded; but they
were never in the remotest degree menaced by the origi-
nators of this good project.”’

The beautiful grounds of St. Kevin’s Park, Kilmacud,
Co. Dublin, presented a scene of great life on Monday
last.. As our readers know, the work carried on in St.
Kevin’s is of great importance to Ireland. One of the
chief departments of work is the training of well-to-do
girls—the class of girls who go to convent boarding
schools—in practical Domestic Economy. And no one
with eyes who mixes about in the Irish middle-
class world can conceal from himself, though he may be
too polite to talk about it, that it would be a good thing
for all concerned if the Irish middle-class girl had, abt
least, one year taken off tho finishing’’ process ab the
select convent hoarding school, and was sent for a year
instead to somo place where she would receive a year’s
course in practical Domestic Bconomy. The feo for full
courses of training for the scholastic year, st September
to 31st July, is only £35, and that, wo consider, is very
reasonable.

However, considerations about Domosbiq Economy
did not disturb the minds of the visitors of Kilmacud on
Monday last. There were an deproeacc asup Scopui-
neacc there. The band of the Church St. Boy’s Brigade
was there; there were open-air concerts, and the Keat-
ings played the Central Branch in the game of Camo-
‘surveact.  On the programme it was ulszl) announced
—“Tennis and croquet all the afternoon.’ ‘When we
saw them playing croquet wo thought ib would be a

% " THE LEADER.

JuNE 9, 1906.

nice game for Chanel,”’ for whom hurling is too
violent. And why should he not play it? Tennis, too,
had better be dealt with, for attempting to ignore it is
only futile. As a game it is, we understand, a very
good game; and it has the great advantage that two
can play it. No kind of life—national or other—can
grow on negatives or “anti’s.”’ Ircland has no tradi-
tional game that supplies the want which tennis sup-
plies. After all, tennis is only a sort of 'hzmd])all, a
traditional Trish game, but handball requires a court
whilo the paraphernalia of tennis is portable.

The mind of Irish Ireland is somewhat Flamlet-like
concerning certain games like tennis. To play or not to
play, that is the question.  The Irish objection to
tennis is largely comprised in the fact that the game
is associated with West Britons, and that if a stray
unit wishes to play i, he is, of necessity, t}n‘oyn|
amongst West Britons. And that is a greab objection
so long as it remains a fact. But if tennis cannot, or
ought not, be killed, and if it is associated with West
Britons, the statesmanlike thing would seem to be to
create such a state of affairs that it will not be asso-
ciated with West Britons. Perhaps some branch of
the Gaelic League would tempt fate by establishing a
tennis club? There are bold spirits who would attempt
to solve the University difficulty by urging that Catho-
lics should go into Trinity, and fight for their hand.
Is there not more to be said for Irish Ireland going into
the tennis ground, and making the game its own, and
not leaving it. as a monopoly to West Britons and
straggling units of Treland who like the game, and who
will play it in spite of newspaper articles resolutions,
and what not?

Take the case of St. Kevin’s Park. The spirit per-
vading the place is very Irish as things go in Ireland.
Would it be reasonable to demand that they should
plough up their tennis court, and burn their rackets,
or else be shouted at as West Britons! If it is not
reasonable, why not formally recognise the game? It
is rather unsatisfactory for Irish Irelanders to ¢ wink’’
at the game while they are supposed to be against it;
it is like passing a resolution that no one intends to
keep. It is quite plain that a mere “anti’’-attitude
has not been, and probably will not be, effective against
tennis. Games, after all, like Irish industrial products
havo to face competition, and the best tend to hold the
market. Ireland did not invent bicycles, yet we all
ride them because they supply a real want; and
billiards is not an Irish game, and yet it is popular.
We have heard that.tennis is really an Irish game.
Not being learned in that class of lore, we can offer no
opinion on the assertion. But the present situation, in
which a popular and interesting game that many good
Irvish people will play no matter what resolutions are
passed, is handed over to the  Johnnies,”” and the West
Briton, is not satisfactory.

Our one time sober contemporary, the Zr::
s‘cad, appeared to be quite drunk with temper last week.
And the amusing thing is that it is the stodgy Freeman
that has put, or that it says has put, it beside itself.
Whoever wields the editorial pen appears to be in such
a state of nervousness that he is either possessed of a
hatred of thought, == suffers from an incapacity to think.
We have vead wordy vhetoric in owr day, but judging by
the notes headed “ The Attacks on Co-operation ’’ in our
ono time sober contemporary, the editor could give points
to Mr. High Faluter in “eloquence.” If our sober
contemporary can’t keep caol it ought to keep as cool as
it can. Tho editor evidently lacks the repose of Vere
de Vere, though, for all we know to the contrary, he
may bo an aristocrat—by adoption. We read “The
gombeen men, jobbers, commission men, and all that lot,
add their voice in council meetings and in the Press to
help Mr. Dillon in the House.” TFollowing a torrent of
rhetoric we read: “That is the game which our wise
Parliamentarians are playing. Behind them and pull-
ing the strings are higglers, publicans, gombeen men.
{obbers, and every kind of disreputable and backward

fluence.”  Evidently our sober contemporary has got
bad attack! Later on it indignantly asks: Is Mr.
,1_Dll‘lon a })uﬁter expert?’  We don’t know. We wonder
is the editor of our sober contemporary one? Develop-
ing its wrath as it goes on it raves in italics: ¢ Let
every kind of creature who can advertise in the * Free-
man ° flourish exceedingly.”” Furthor on we read “ Co-
operative societics don’t need the aid of that Press to
reach their customers. Therefore he cry of the anti-co-
opsrative advertiser dictates the policy of the anti-co-
operative paper. There is no higher national, politic,
or patriotic motive than that.”” = What an exquisite
subject for comedy is the man who is conscientiously
convinced that everyone is a rogue but himself! We
suppose our sober contemporary would take it ill if the
Deplorable—-if the Deplorable had humour enough to do
so—asked the cditor of our contemporary— And how
much a week do you get from the Department or other
source for writing all that sort of stuff!”’

The secretary of the Killeshandra (Co. Cavan) Co-
operative Agricultural and Dairy Society, Ltd., has been
requested by his committec to send us the following re-
solution for publication : —“ Killeshandra Co-operative
Dairy and Agricultural Society, Ltd.—“ At a meeting
i of the Managing Committee of the above society, held

\ on Wednesday, May 30th, 1906, the following resolution
? was proposed by Mr. Flood, seconded by Mr. Leddy, and

unanimously carried:—'‘ That we, as representing one
of the largest dairy societies in Ireland, with a member-

'\‘,: ship of 1,400 farmers, hereby protest in the strongest
3 possible manner against the action of the Department of
i Agriculture and Technical Instruction for Ireland, and

the Irish Agricultural Organisation Society, as shown in
the questions of Mr. Dillon, and the answers of the Chief
Secretary thereto, reported in the Press on May 25th,
and previous dates. The forwer for making large pay-
i ments (£18,000) of public money to an irresponsible phil-
anthropic association, which till 1904 was not respon-
sible to, or elected by the co-operative societics in Ire-
fand, and concealing from the societies the fact that
these hluge payments had been made, and so preventing
+hem from insisting on some control of funds, supposed
f to be supplied, for their benefit. With regard to the
action of the I.A.O.S. in the matter, we consider it
1 unfuir and discreditable, for neither in its annual re-
| ports, or in its organ, the Irish Homestead, during the
] past five years, is there one word to inform the societies
) that about £4,000 per annum had been received by them
| from the Department in aid of agricultural co-operation ;
i

|

|

in fact, many statements have been made by the Zome-
stead and the I.A.0.S. officials, tending distinctly to
mislead the societies in this respect. As the Killeshan-
dra Society declines to affiliate with the I.A.0.S., as it
disapproves of its methods, and only affiliated socicties
ara helped by them, we strongly object to any further
payments heing made by the Department to this Asso-
ciation, unless the Department is prepared to make pro-
portionate grants to the many unaffiliated societics
throughout the country. We also request that a public
statement shall be given of the salaries and expenses paid
by the I.A.0.S. during the period it was receiving these
grants from this good Department.”

- Trom the above it will be seen that there is co-opera-
tive activity that is not comnected directly with the
1.A.0.8. We are advocates of all sorts of beneficial co-
operation, and as we belicve that distribution is rather
overdone in Ireland, no argument that a fow huxters
shops would suffer by co-operative developmont would
intimidate us in the least.. Co-operation in buying and
selling on ths part of farmers is a now machine, and a
beneficial one, and the contention that the introduction
of such a néw machine might injure the chances of som:>
old machines should not be permitted to stop tthe tido of
g One may not only believe in co-operation bub
‘practise it, and yob not be prepared to swear that the
~ sun rises out of 22 Lincoln Place, Dublin.

e:" no far distant date’’ cannot now be far off, for
ad in the London correspondence of Alf Foz:—

THE LEADER. 243

«My. Mooney, M.P., drew Spearmint in the Derby
Sweep at the National Liberal Club, and congequently
won the first prize of £70.”” We wonder did young
Mooney “change’’ the £70 at once, and ?;r_eat Pat
0’Bricn and Tim Harvington to bottles of British-made
hop bitters in one of the Mooney shops!

The Loughrea cviction and fight are incidents
for which the Town Tenants’ League ougitc (o
bo, and no doubt are, thankful. Mr. T. W. Russell
said:— He (Mr. Russell) knew Clanricarde and his
agent, who wero thoe two danger points in the whole of'
that community. It was a very terrible thing (:I_la,t one
man—an absentee from the country, doing nothing but
drawing £20,000 a year out of the poor people of five
or six baronies, without giving sixpence to any charit-
ablo thing—should be allowed to light a fire that might
consume all the good fecling created in Ireland in past
years. The time of the House of Commons would be
well spent in passing a measure for the expropriation
of the Marquis of Clanricarde.” Clanricarde and the
man Tener are mere Sourfaces, and, like a lot of other
Sourfaces, they would like to wipe the face of Ireland
clean of ““rebelly Papists.”” Tener tried to intimidate
Mr. Ward one way; various other Teners are carrying
on a similar game all over the country, and adapt their
tactics to the varying circumstances. The Great Northern
Railway Co. play the game in their own way. The
amiable and very “ sympathetic > Chief Secretary, when
challenged about the Loughrea business on ¢ the floor,””
replied sympathetically. But what could he do? Poor
man! “The Executive,” said he, ““in matters of this
kind is a mere piece of machinery, and has no more dis-
cretion than a mere machine set in motion by the turn-
ing of a handle or a lever.”” It has been said that the
law is a h-ass. A simple problem is—If the law is a
h-ass, what is the Exccutive that is a mere piece of
machinery without discretion, and subject to the wag
of the tail and the stamp of the hoof of an ass? The
Chief Secretary said : —“ Nothing would give me greater
pleasure than to put pressure of that kind upon Lord
Clanricarde, but I am afraid anyone who knows him
will think that pressure exercised by me would not be
likely to be effective.”

If the town tenants were looking for a case that would
underline, and doubly underline, their agitation, they
could not find a better one than Clanricarde supplied to
them. Clanricarde owns the site of Loughrea, so that
if a Loughrea business man is dispossessed, he may be
cvicted out of the town as well as out of the house.
Clanricarde, through his agent, Tener, attempted to
play the part of tyrant—a part that is not so easily
played to-day as twenty years ago. As our readers
know, Mr. Ward became secretary of the local branch of
the United Irish League, and Tencr, evidently not lik-
ing him in that capacity, gave him notice to quit. The
whole thing reads like a belated chapter from the early
cighties. IHowever, the town tenants may regard the
whole proceedings as being worth years of organisatiou
on b:half of the League to which they belong.

In the course of our references last weck to Dr.
Hogan’s pamphlet, we referred to some streaks of
apology here and there which grated upon us.  Dr.
Abbott, one of the old fossils of the Robber University,
roplied to some part of the pamphlet. It is one thing
being too timid and apologetic; it is another to be
“cheeky ”’ like a corner-boy. But then Dr. Abbott is
a Senior Fellow of the mud-throwing University, and
what ‘can you expect? We have little sympathy with
Dr. Hogan in the matter, though that does not affect
our opinion of the Trinity Senior Fellow. Referring to
something which he alleges Dr. Hogan said concerning
tho evidence of one Foley, Dr. Abbott says:— Foley’s
answer was all true; this is all false.”” ~ There is not
much kid glove about that. But we are surprised at
Dr. Abbott’s comparative good manners. Why did he
not, in good, downright Trinity cad style, state that Dr.
Hogan was ‘“a d——d liar,”” and have done with it?
But perhaps he was afraid that if he expressed himself

" in that Trinity cad style that his lotter might not be
inserted!  The last paragraph of the Trinity polite
letter-writer’s epistle is as follows:—“T believe I have
not exhausted all the errors in these fow pages. I
have not curiosity to read the rest of the pamphlet.”’
Perhaps that fish-wife sentence may do something in the
. way of showing Dr. Hogan the futility of bowing and
scraping to Fellows, young or anciont, of the Robber
}Jmvers.\ty. It is plain, anyway, that, notwithstanding
its faults, the pamphlet of Dr. Hogan has roused the
ire of Dr. Abbott, of the Spelling University.  Bvi-
I d.ently the manners inculcated in the Robber Univer-
sity are not above suspicion any more than the spelling

or the medical examinations,

A Nenagh correspondent writes:— Nenagh Churis-
tian young Men’s ~ Association.—¢ No Cmt,hoglics 110’:(1
apply.” Such is the fundamental rule of this associa-
tion wherein Catholics are not eligible for membership.
A concert was held in the Town Hall, Nenagh, on Wed-
nesday and .Thm'sd.'l,y last by the Nenagh Orchestral
and Dramatic Society. The honorary officials and Com-
mittes of this society are exclusively Protestant. They
organised and held a concert in aid of the debt on the
hall into which no Catholics dare set foot. The concert
programme reveals to the writer 22 advertisements, 16
are of the Prince Catholic merchants of Nenagh in aid of
an institution that they are not eligible for membership
by reason of their creed. I won’t comment on the form
of advertisements beyond a Leaguer’s advertisement in
styling himself as the proprietor of ‘The Birmingham
and Sheflield Warehouse,” which is centrally situated in
the capital town of North Tipperary. The concert pro-
gramme consisted of a farce, entitled ‘Borrowed
Plumes,’ an appropriate farce for the occasion, as the
audience composed farcical Catholic plumes borrowed to
help to free the debt of a hall they cannot enter unless
they abjure their faith. The second part of the pro-
gramme was made up of seven artistes, two of whom
are genteel ‘ Cawtholics * who never miss an opportunity
of rubbing skirts with the so-called upper ten, and the
concert most reverently terminated with ¢ God Save the
King.’ ”

The third annual Kilkenny Feis was a great success
notwithstanding the weather. There were a great
many book-prizes given in connection with the competi-
tions.  Amongst the book-prizes were ‘‘Seaona,”
“Tom O’Kelly,” “Citt-dipne,” ¢ Philosophy of
Irish Ireland,” “.An Cpaop Oearhan.’” We notice
that the first, second and third places in the competi-
tions for juniors for Irish story-telling were won by
pupils taught by Miss Cronin, and the winner of the
Irish recitation competition for juniors was also a pupil
of the same teacher. Pupils of the Christian Brothers’
Schools, James’s Street, Kilkenny, won the inter-class
competition. During the course of the concert in the
evening, Captain Cuffe stated that the examiner in the
literary competitions expressed the opinion that candi-
dates at the Feis were the best in any non-Irish-speak-
ing district that he knew of in Ireland.

The Newbridge College Magazine comes to hand as we
prepare for Press. It is the first in the field of College
magazines. We have had only timo to hurriedly turn
the pages. There are several articles in Irish, and in
make-up and appearanco this year’s magazine is an im-
g:gement on previous issues, and that is saying a good

Our readers will be glad to hear that the efforts of Mr.
Hayden, 9 Eden Quay, Dublin, to get financial backing
for the projected boot factory on American lines in
Dublin, are meeting with very considerablo success.
Am_on those who have promised to subscribe somo
capital is Mr, William O’Brien, M.P.  Perhaps Mr.
John Dillon might be induced to follow suit. Several
gentlemen connected with large business houses that
deal in boots have put down their names as subscribers ;
and all, or nearly all, the big howses in Dublin have
ivon promises of support in the way of purchasing

00 THE LEADER.

Juse 9, 1906,

the products of the factory. Amongst those who are
taking a keen interest in the undertaking is Mr. W. P.
Ryan, C.E., F.S.I., a well-known London-Irish sur-
voyor and valuer. Mr. Hayden has samples of the
boots which it is intended to manufacture; and Mr.
Hegarty is prepared to come over from America as soon
as preliminaries are arranged. A private provisional
prospectus will probably be issued in a fortnight or so,
and after that a meeting of the promised shareholders
will bo called, at which a full statement of Mr. Hegarty’s
proposals will bo submitted, and the selection of a Board
of Dircctors, the issuing of a prospectus and such
matters will be discussed. We will keep our readers in
touch with the developments as weo hear of them.

A reader in England writes:— Last week I sent t?
an Irish draper for patterns of ‘Irish manufacture.
In duc time they arrived, but not as I had specified.
The samples contained two pieces of Irish, and nine of
English, manufacture. He imagined, I suppose, I was
so devoid of common sense as to send to Ireland for
what is produced hero. This gentleman hails from the
City of the Confederation, where they opened the
woollen mills a short time ago amidst such a blare of
trumpets. It augurs well for the success of that Irish
industry when a ‘ confederate’ trys to push the sale of
foreign goods in this fashion. I beg to compliment you
on the splendid fight you are making for Justice, Toler-
ance and Fair Play. The perusal of the LEADER each
week is to me a genuine pleasure.”

It is significant that the first and second prizes for
junior recitation (girls) at the Dublin Feis were won by
two pupils of the Marlborough St. Schools. Both
these little girls, we hear, were taught Irish at Marl-
borough Street School at the special request of their
parents. Now, if some parents of Marlborough Street
Schools see that their children are taught Irish, why do
not parents who have children in other schools assert
themselves?  Probably in some schools the parents
would have to request vigorously, for whilst a frown
from an eye-glassed, half-pay officer might be enough to
convince the authorities of some of our schools that
Irish was no “class,”” it would, perhaps, be necessary
for the parents in favour of Irish to combine, and
threaten to withdraw their boys from the school if they
wanted to make an impression.

There is a mild row going on over the appointment of
Seumar O Ve  as medical officer in Oughterard.
Mr. P. W. Anketel Jones trotted up to Dublin, and
took the opinion of the Local Government Board on
the matter. Who asked P. Willoughby Anketel Jones,
a P.L.G. of Oughterard, to run up with a “Please,
sir”’ yarn to the L.G.B.? The L.G.B. did not inter-
fere with the advertisement, which stated that the

medical officer should have a thorough knowledge of -

Irish. What business have they interfering now that
the vacancy has been filled in accordance with the terms
of the advertisement? The Chairman, Mr. O’Malley,
objected to the action of the L.G.B. in refus-
ing sanction to the appointment made by the Board.
By ten votes to six it was decided, despite the inter-
ference of the L.G.B., to stand by the appointment
already made. Mr, P. Willoughby Anketel Jones had
betber come up to, tho L.G.B. again, and tell on his
colleagues once more.

THE PASSING OF DAVITT.

T is not an exaggeration to say that not only Ire-
1 land, but the Irish race, mourns the death of
Michael Davitt. He was a brave man, whatever his
views; when we place his character in relief against
that of high-placed men nurtured from their cradles on
all that money and influence can buy, and contemplate
the son of the evicted peasant, losing his arm whilst yet
a boy, going through the awful experience of penal ser-
vitude, we see, and the world is better of seeing it, what
grit can do agaiust all odds. The problems of Ireland’s
future lics largely in the question of grit or no grit, and
in the career of the Mayo peasant boy, whose bones
to-day rest peaceably with those of his ancestors in an
obscure country churchyard, there is a vivid answer to
those who deny masterful qualities to the Irish
character.

Irish youths take to politics early.  We are old
cnough to recall some of the “ atmosphere ’’ of the old
Land League days, when the names—Parnell, Dillon,
Davitt, with Rossa and Stephens in the background—
summed up in our minds the trend and scope of Irish
national endeavour. Nationality was politics with a
vengeance in those days. We have read in one of the
newspaper accounts of Michael Davitt’s caveer, the
text of the resolutions which he drafted and proposed
ab the famous Irishtown meeting—a meeting which is
generally looked back to as the source of the Land
League. At that time—some twenby-seven years ago—
that part of tho County Mayo must have been very
much an Irish-speaking district, yet the language of the
resolutions is in the height of “fine writing’’ in Eng-
lish, and it must have been in great part a mere jumble
of moro or less foreign words to the great gathering of
Mayo peasants on that historic occasion. There was no
idoa abroad then of cultivating Ireland from its own
Trish roots; the fight was against landlordism. The ex-
termination of landlords overshadowed every other
issue. Yet landlordism was an institution which was
anti-Trish only by accident, for it was an institution
that might conceivably have been Irish, and in the
latter case the struggle against it might have had no-
thing whatever to do with nationality. “Down with
tho landlords!”” was then the creed of Irish nationality.

‘Wo can, of course, understand how the situation came

about, we can sympathise with those who played the

great parts in it, even though we are alive to its glaring
imperfections. “An Irish Irelander twenty-five years ago
would have been harangued at as a “red herring,’” and
 would have b_oon trampled upon as an insect, Unfor-
' tunately Davitt’s English schooling—such as it was—
and his life in England, appear to have so -Ampressed
him that he never rang in completo harmony with oven
+ the Ircland of his day; there was frequently a dis-
cordant note. He became enamoured of tho fascinat-
ing, but rather cheap, theory of land nationalisation
the while the Land League, stripped of all its national
and other embroideries, meant to the Irish tenant
farmers (zh‘e land for owrselves.”” The Land League
was a necessity, but it incidentally bred boycotting, and
the spirit of boycotting has sunk deeply into the
country to the great detriment of the cause of honest
individual liberty; and it will take many years to get
the effects out of the national system; let us admit
it, it even taints the blood of Irish Ireland. Davitt
was not an Irish Irelander; he more pertained to be a
citizen of the world, and he had a regard for the British
democracy which the average Irish Nationalist finds it
impossible to share,

i
i

call them so, are not Irish Irclanders, and they are
now too settled or too old to change. If Davitt never
grasped the Irish Ireland point of view, neither has
Dillon, nor Sexton, nor O’Brien. Healy, in the course of
a recent speech at the University College, paid his
Tespects to it, but he is gone beyond the time of new
enthusiasm ; he is now a successful lawyer, and a tired
and disillusioned politician.

The Freeman, in its editorial article on Davitt,
said : —*“ From that day at Irishtown the cause of Ire-
land has gone marching on.”” Of course, the truth is,
and we are willing to admit that the Freeman in this
connection may be suffering from what the theologians
call “invincible ignorance *’—the truth is, the cause of
Ireland has been rapidly going downhill since 1879,
and since a far earlier period. Davitt, however, played
an heroic and a gritty part according to his lights, and
Ireland will honour his memory; and the story of his
life will stimulate many an Irishman and Irishwoman
yet unborn.

Truly, the landlords -have been the great curse of the
Irish race in modern times; and it is aggravating to
find how easily some of them, by uttering a few words
of cheap sympathy, can impose on a too credulous
people. Notwithstanding the havoc they have wrought,
what are they attempting to do by way of reparation to
an outraged nation, now that they are getting fancy
prices and a bonus for the Irish people’s land? How
many industries have they started?  Where are they
placing the money they have received? One of their
breed, Clanricarde, now that he has got half a chance,
is doing his Sourface, anti-Irish work at Loughrea. In
Dayvitt’s time the landlords had their alien heels on the

Before the Act of 1723, the Trustees founded in
Trinity College 20 Exhibitions of £8 each, and 15 Ex-
hibitions of £6 each—open to, or to be given to, all the
students in Trinity College. This was a breach of Trust.
For Erasmus Smith having directed in the Indenture
that Exhibitions of £10 should be given to the tenants’
childron for four years in Trinity College, added:—
“Provided that, in the first place, the children of the
tenants and inhabitants of the said lands, and other
lands of the said Erasmus Smith and his heirs in Ire-

land, to be first provided for, and after them
such as shall be cducated in the said schools,
being poor, and for want of such, for the

relief of such other poor scholars as in the judgment of
the said Trustees (provided there be no fewer than 7 of
‘thom to consult . . .) shall be held fit t3 receive
tho same, provided always that the two latter sort of
poor shall give place to the former as they shall grow up,
and be fit to be entered in the said College.””

Thus, the Exhibitions are to be given in the first place
to the children of the tenants and inhabitants of the
said lands, and it is only when there are not children of
the tenants or inhabitants of the said lands fit for, and
desiring the Exhibitions, that it is lawful to give them
to “ other poor children educated in the said schools.’”
And it is only when there is no child of either of the
classes just mentioned fit for, and desiring, an Exhibi-
tion, that it is permitted to give it to a poor scholar, who
is neither a tenant’s son nor inhabitant’s son, educated in
the said schools. And if a poor scholar, not a tenant’s
or inhabitant’s son, who has been educated in any of the
said schools, has got an Exhibition, he must surrender it
to a tenant’s or inhabitant’s son as soon as he is fit
for it, and desires it. And the person of the third class
who has got an Exhibition must surrender it also as soon
as there is a tenant’s or inhabitant’s son fit for, and desir-
ing, the same. This is expressly stated in the Letters
Patent, and is.the meaning of the words of the Inden-
ture :—“ Provided always that the two latter sort of
poor shall give place to the former as soon as they shall
grow up and be fit to be entered in the said College.”

The Letters Patent are, as I have said, more distinct in
their directions on this point. They say:—“ And upon
further trust and confidence, that always after the said
salaries payed and deduccons of reasonable charges and
expences of the said Henry Jones, etc., they shall im-
ploy all the residue of rents and profitts of the premises
in pencons or exhibicons to such poor scholars to be edu-
cated in the said schooles as shall become students in the
said University not exceeding £8 a-piece,
yearly, by the space of seven years, from their respee-
tive admissions into the said University, and
for want of such to other poor scholars in the University
aforesaid to be nominated by the said Henry
Jones, ctc. or the major part of them. . . .
Provided always that the children of poore Inhabitants
on the lands of the said Erasmus Smith, his heirs and
assignes, shall, upon request, be preferred to said yearly
pensions before any other, and in case any
other be prefferred to any of the said pensions . . .
when none of the said poore Inhabitants’ children shall
bee fit for, or desire, the same, such pension shall cease
to such other, when any child or children of such poore
Inhabitants shall be fit for, and desire, the same, who
shall have the same according.’

It is ovident, therefore, that the establishment of 35
Exhibitions all at one stroke was a breach of trust. For
the Letters Patent expressly divect that all the residue
was to be “employed in Exhibitions to such poore
scholars to be educated in the said schools, as shall be-

If they had unlimited power to establish Exhibitions
“out of the whole revenue,”” they might have estab-
lished Exhibitions in Trinity College out of more than
£9,000 a year, by which the revenues of the Endowment
have since increased. They had just as much authority to

in Trinity College, as they had to expend in 35 Exhibi-

revenues had increased at some time anterior to 1723.

The Act of 1723 gave the Governors of the Erasmus
Smith Schools no authority or legal power to pay for
those Exhibitions in Trinity. Therefore their payment
for them has been illegal, and a breach of trust, down to
the present day.

The truth of the first proposition will be manifest
from a brief examination of the Act of 1723.
